title: Dreaming Across Towns: Semantic Rollout and Town-Adversarial Regularization for Zero-Shot Held-Out-Town Fixed-Route Driving in CARLA
abstract: 经过训练的驾驶智能体在部署到未见过的环境时，性能往往会退化。本文在CARLA模拟器中研究了一个刻意受限的实例问题：从Town05和Town06向未见过的Town03和Town04进行零样本迁移的闭环固定路线驾驶智能体。该研究通过固定天气为ClearNoon并移除行人与交通流，隔离了结构性城镇变化因素。我们基于Dreamer风格的潜在世界模型智能体，增加了两项仅在训练阶段使用的辅助损失函数：沿想象轨迹预测未来视觉语义嵌入的多步时序任务，以及对循环潜在状态语义投影施加的城镇对抗性监督。因果上下文特征对语义轨迹预测器进行条件约束，而演员与评论家网络仍使用标准控制特征。策略无需接收导航指令、路线折线、目标位姿或地图输入；参考路线仅由环境用于奖励计算、进度评估、成功判定与终止条件。在评估的未接触城镇中，所提模型在Dreamer系列方法中取得了最高平均成功率。次要的安全性与车道保持指标在不同城镇间表现各异。这些结果支持一个有限的结论：在该受控固定天气的CARLA设定下，语义轨迹预测监督结合城镇对抗正则化能够提升未接触城镇的路线完成率均值。

Dreaming Across Towns: Semantic Rollout and Town-Adversarial Regularization for Zero-Shot Held-Out-Town Fixed-Route Driving in CARLA
Feeza Khan Khanzada, 
and Jaerock Kwon
Feeza Khan Khanzada, and Jaerock Kwon are with the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, University of Michigan–Dearborn, Dearborn, MI 48128 USA (e-mail: feezakk@umich.edu; jrkwon@umich.edu).
Abstract
Learned driving agents often degrade when deployed in unseen environments. This paper studies a deliberately bounded instance of that problem in the CARLA simulator: zero-shot transfer of a closed-loop fixed-route driving agent from Town05 and Town06 to unseen Town03 and Town04. The study isolates structural town shift by keeping weather fixed to ClearNoon and removing traffic and pedestrians. We build on a Dreamer-style latent world-model agent and add two training-only auxiliary losses: multi-horizon prediction of future visual-semantic embeddings along imagined rollouts and town-adversarial supervision on a semantic projection of the recurrent latent state. A causal context feature conditions the semantic rollout predictor, while the actor and critic retain the standard control feature. The policy receives no navigation command, route polyline, goal pose, or map input; the reference route is used only by the environment for reward, progress, success, and termination. Across the evaluated held-out towns, the proposed model achieves the highest mean success rate among the included Dreamer-family methods. Secondary safety and lane-keeping metrics are mixed across towns. These results support a bounded conclusion: in this controlled fixed-weather CARLA setting, semantic rollout supervision combined with town-adversarial regularization improves mean held-out-town route completion.
I
Introduction
Learned driving agents often degrade when deployed in environments that differ from those seen during training. This paper studies a deliberately bounded instance of that problem in the CARLA simulator
[
16
]
i.e zero-shot held-out-town transfer for route-unconditioned fixed-route closed-loop driving. Training uses only Town05 and Town06, and evaluation is performed without target-domain adaptation on unseen Town03 and Town04 under fixed ClearNoon weather and without traffic or pedestrians.
The scope is deliberately restricted. Training is performed on source towns only, and evaluation is performed in held-out towns without target-domain adaptation. Weather is held fixed, and the environment contains only the ego vehicle and its attached sensors, without traffic or pedestrians. The goal is therefore not to claim broad robustness to all forms of autonomous-driving distribution shift, but to test whether a learned route-following agent can retain route-relevant competence when town layout and lane topology change.
Existing strategies only partially address this problem. Image augmentation can improve invariance to low-level appearance changes, but it does not explicitly regularize imagined future representations. Domain-adversarial learning
[
18
]
can reduce town-predictive information, and semantic supervision can encourage route-relevant structure, but either mechanism alone may be insufficient. This is therefore a route-unconditioned fixed-route setting rather than a command-conditioned or map-conditioned urban driving benchmark.
We study this question within a DreamerV3-style
[
22
]
world-model reinforcement-learning framework. We augment the agent with two training-time auxiliary objectives: multi-horizon prediction of frozen OpenCLIP
[
9
]
image embeddings along imagined rollouts, and town-adversarial supervision on a semantic projection of the deterministic RSSM state. A history-conditioned context feature conditions the semantic rollout predictor. In the primary model, the actor and critic retain the standard Dreamer feature, so the semantic/context branches act as auxiliary regularizers rather than replacing the control input. These auxiliary signals are used only during training; deployment in held-out towns is zero-shot and uses no further adaptation.
Our claims are deliberately bounded. We do not claim robustness to weather, traffic, pedestrians, sensor corruption, or real-world deployment. Instead, we evaluate whether these training-time auxiliaries improve held-out-town transfer in a fixed-weather CARLA route-following setting using task-completion, safety, lane-keeping, and speed metrics.
The contributions of this paper are threefold.
•
We define a controlled CARLA protocol for zero-shot held-out-town fixed-route driving that isolates structural shift in town layout and lane topology by fixing weather and removing traffic and pedestrians.
•
We augment a Dreamer-style latent world-model agent with two training-only auxiliaries: multi-horizon prediction of future visual-semantic embeddings and town-adversarial regularization on a semantic latent projection.
•
Within this benchmark, the auxiliary-only semantic design achieves the highest mean held-out-town success among the included Dreamer-family methods, while direct semantic-policy control is weaker.
The comparisons below are descriptive mean ± standard deviation across three independently trained seeds; the paper does not claim formal statistical significance from these runs alone.
II
Related Work
This section reviews prior work most relevant to the present study: learning-based autonomous driving, world-model-based driving, domain-invariant representation learning, structured representations for transfer, vision-language supervision, and CARLA evaluation protocols.
II-A
Learning-based Autonomous Driving Policies
End-to-end driving policies learn a direct mapping from observations to control, often with additional conditioning or structure to mitigate ambiguity. Conditional Imitation Learning (CIL) conditions policies on high-level commands to reduce multimodality in demonstrations and improve controllability across maneuvers
[
11
]
. Learning by Cheating (LbC) leverages privileged information available in simulation to train a privileged “teacher” and distill behavior into a vision-based student, aiming to inherit robustness while deploying only standard sensory inputs
[
7
]
. Related efforts seek to reduce reliance on dense labels by learning compact visual abstractions that support driving control with less annotation burden
[
2
]
. In parallel, RL-based driving has been explored as an alternative to imitation when rewards and interaction are available, including recent end-to-end RL formulations in urban driving settings
[
13
]
. Hybrids that combine imitation with reinforcement learning objectives have also been proposed to exploit demonstration priors while still improving through interaction
[
5
]
. These works primarily improve the policy mapping from perception to control, using supervision, RL, or hybrid objectives. In contrast, our paper studies how latent structure inside a world-model agent affects zero-shot transfer under held-out-town shift.
II-B
World Models and Latent Planning for Driving
World models aim to learn a compact latent dynamics representation that enables planning, imagination, or model-based value learning from high-dimensional observations. Early latent dynamics approaches for planning from pixels established the core idea: learn a probabilistic latent transition model and plan in latent space
[
21
]
. Dreamer-style agents operationalize this by training a world model and optimizing behavior via imagined rollouts, enabling model-based RL without explicit online planning at every step
[
20
]
. DreamerV3 scales this paradigm across diverse domains through architectural and optimization refinements, strengthening the case for general-purpose world-model agents
[
22
]
. In autonomous driving, Think2Drive
[
28
]
formulates CARLA-v2 driving as model-based reinforcement learning with a latent world model used as a neural simulator for planning, illustrating the relevance of latent imagination for driving control.
Within autonomous driving, multiple works explore how to instantiate world-model ideas under the constraints of closed-loop control, safety-critical dynamics, and rich visual scenes. Learning to Drive from a World on Rails uses learned predictive structure to support driving-relevant reasoning under constrained dynamics and perception
[
6
]
. MILE jointly learns a world model and a driving policy for urban driving and reports transfer to unseen towns and weather, making it a close driving-specific precedent for world-model-based transfer
[
24
]
. SEM2 likewise argues for filtering world-model features toward driving-relevant semantics through a semantic masked recurrent world model
[
19
]
. More recent driving-focused world-model systems argue for aligning learned dynamics with driving semantics and controllable factors; for example, Raw2Drive formulates end-to-end autonomous driving in a model-based RL framework and emphasizes aligned world models for sample efficiency and performance under varying conditions
[
36
]
.
Prior driving world-model papers mainly emphasize predictive fidelity, planning efficiency, or control performance. Our paper instead studies whether semantic rollout regularization and town-adversarial regularization on a semantic projection improve zero-shot transfer under held-out-town shift within a DreamerV3-style route-following agent.
II-C
Domain Shift, Domain Randomization, and Adversarial Invariance
Robustness to domain shift is a central theme in learning-based robotics. Domain randomization popularized the strategy of varying textures, lighting, and other simulator parameters during training to encourage invariance and transfer
[
33
]
. Dynamics randomization extends this idea to physical parameters, showing that broadening the range of simulated dynamics can improve transfer of control policies
[
30
]
. When explicit randomization is insufficient or expensive, adaptation mechanisms aim to infer latent environment parameters online. Rapid Motor Adaptation (RMA) learns to adapt behavior via a learned latent “context” inferred from recent history, supporting fast adaptation to changing dynamics
[
27
]
.
Another family of approaches targets domain shift by enforcing domain invariance in learned representations. Domain-adversarial training uses a gradient reversal layer to learn features predictive for the task but uninformative about the domain
[
17
]
. Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) formalizes the goal of learning predictors that remain optimal across environments, motivating invariance as a principle for out-of-distribution generalization
[
1
]
. For RL specifically, domain adaptation has been studied through shared representations or unified latent dynamics across domains, aiming to transfer behaviors learned in one environment to another
[
35
]
.
Our setting is domain generalization within simulation under held-out-town shift. Rather than broadening the training distribution through randomization, we retain a fixed training distribution and apply domain-adversarial supervision to a semantic projection inside a DreamerV3-style world model. A history-conditioned context feature conditions the semantic rollout predictor, while the actor and critic retain the standard Dreamer feature. Relative to prior adversarial and invariance-based methods, the contribution here is the placement of these constraints inside a driving world model for held-out-town transfer.
II-D
Structured Representations for Transfer
Disentangled representation learning seeks factors that correspond to distinct underlying generative causes, with the hope that separating stable content from nuisance variation improves transfer. Domain Separation Networks explicitly split representations into shared and private components to isolate domain-invariant features from domain-specific ones
[
3
]
. InfoGAN introduces an information-theoretic objective to encourage latent variables to capture interpretable factors in generative models
[
8
]
. In robotics and RL, DARLA argues that learning disentangled representations (with unsupervised objectives) before policy learning can improve zero-shot transfer
[
23
]
. Further work studies how factorization choices and inductive biases impact disentanglement properties
[
26
]
, and how identifiability constraints can be used to make learned factors more stable and useful under domain shifts
[
29
]
. More recently, “disentangled world models” have been proposed to transfer semantic knowledge by separating semantics from other visual variation inside predictive models
[
34
]
.
Our method is related to this line of work in that it introduces structure into the latent representation, but it does not claim a full semantic–style disentanglement result. Instead, it uses a semantic projection of the deterministic RSSM state and a separate history-conditioned context feature that conditions semantic rollout prediction. The goal is pragmatic: encourage route-relevant semantics while reducing town-predictive information in the semantic branch.
II-E
Semantic and Vision–Language Priors for Driving Representations
Semantic abstraction is a recurring strategy for robustness: if the representation emphasizes geometry and agents rather than textures or lighting, policies may generalize better. A prominent example is using privileged or structured signals in simulation to learn robust policies that deploy with standard sensors
[
7
]
. More recently, vision–language models (VLMs) such as CLIP provide large-scale semantic priors learned from image–text pairs, enabling semantically meaningful embeddings that can be reused for downstream tasks
[
31
]
. Several robotics and RL works leverage CLIP-like embeddings as supervision or as a mechanism to connect behavior to high-level semantics; RoboCLIP argues that CLIP-aligned representations can reduce demonstration requirements by transferring semantic structure from pretraining
[
32
]
. CLIP-derived similarity has also been used as an RL reward signal to encourage goal-aligned behavior, including in driving
[
15
]
. Under distribution shifts, there is also interest in adapting frozen VLMs efficiently for domain adaptation settings
[
10
]
. Related directions use CLIP as a training signal for temporally grounded motion understanding
[
14
]
.
We use CLIP
[
9
]
differently from reward-shaping approaches. Rather than converting CLIP similarity into reward, we use frozen OpenCLIP image embeddings as multi-horizon semantic targets for imagined rollouts, thereby regularizing the predictive model rather than the policy objective directly.
II-F
Robustness and Evaluation Protocols in Simulated Driving
Evaluation methodology matters disproportionately under distribution shift: small changes in town layout, traffic patterns, or sensing can produce qualitatively different failure modes. CARLA provides a widely used simulation platform for developing and benchmarking autonomous driving policies
[
16
]
, and the CARLA Leaderboard and associated challenges formalize standardized tasks and metrics for closed-loop driving evaluation
[
4
]
. Earlier, NoCrash evaluated generalization to unseen towns and conditions in CARLA and is a more direct precedent for the present held-out-town setting than generic simulator citations alone
[
12
]
. More recently, Bench2Drive and Bench2Drive-R emphasize difficult long-horizon driving scenarios and robustness stress tests to better separate methods that merely fit training distributions from those that generalize
[
25
,
37
]
.
We do not propose a new benchmark or a new planning stack. Instead, this paper combines multi-horizon semantic rollout supervision with town-adversarial regularization inside a DreamerV3-style route-following agent and evaluates that combination under a focused held-out-town CARLA protocol.
III
Methodology
III-A
Problem Formulation and Setting
Figure 1:
Overview of the proposed framework. The model is trained on source-domain rollouts using a world model, a semantic auxiliary branch, a policy-learning branch, and a domain-adversarial branch. A frozen OpenCLIP image encoder provides target semantic embeddings, while the learned latent representation supports imagined rollouts, semantic prediction, and domain-invariant learning. During unseen target domain, the learned policy is deployed directly in an unseen target domain without target-domain adaptation.
We formulate the task as fixed-route closed-loop navigation over predefined start-goal route instances in CARLA. The reference route is used only by the environment to compute progress, reward, success, and termination; no explicit navigation command, route waypoint, goal pose, or map input is provided to the policy. Each episode instantiates a route defined by a start pose and an end pose, and terminates upon success (reaching the goal) or failure conditions (e.g., collision, persistent offroad/wrong-lane, being stuck, or timeout). Throughout this paper, ‘route following’ denotes environment-evaluated completion of predefined fixed routes; it does not imply command-conditioned navigation or route-geometry input to the policy.

Overview of the Proposed Approach
The proposed model augments a DreamerV3-style world model with two training-time auxiliary losses: multi-horizon prediction of frozen OpenCLIP image embeddings and town-adversarial supervision on a semantic projection of the deterministic RSSM state. A history-conditioned context feature summarizes encoder embeddings observed up to time

The source route pools contain 8 fixed routes in Town05 and 8 fixed routes in Town06. These source route pools are used for training. IV-D
Compared Methods and Ablations
All compared agents are implemented within the same DreamerV3-style world-model reinforcement-learning framework and share the same simulator, route sets, action space, camera configuration, replay pipeline, and episode termination conditions. The comparisons differ only in whether the semantic projection and context branch are enabled, whether the actor and critic consume the standard Dreamer feature
f
t
f_{t}
or the semantic feature
s

s_{t}
, and which auxiliary losses are active.
Scope of comparison:
The goal of the empirical comparison is controlled mechanism isolation rather than leaderboard-style cross-paradigm ranking. We therefore keep the simulator, fixed route sets, camera observation interface, action discretization, replay pipeline, and DreamerV3-style world-model backbone fixed, and vary only the latent regularization mechanisms under study. Several strong CARLA systems discussed in Section II belong to materially different problem settings, including command-conditioned imitation policies, privileged-teacher/student pipelines, and planner- or world-model systems evaluated with different conditioning signals, supervision, or benchmark protocols. Evaluating such methods with their original inputs would introduce additional confounds beyond the proposed auxiliaries, while re-engineering them to fit the present route-unconditioned fixed-route interface would materially change the methods themselves. For this reason, the primary comparison is restricted to matched Dreamer-family variants, and broader cross-paradigm benchmarking is left to future work.

We use the following primary comparison methods.
•
Dreamer-Std
: the standard DreamerV3-style agent without RGB augmentation, without visual-semantic supervision, and without domain supervision.
•
Dreamer-Aug
: the same base Dreamer agent trained with image-space data augmentation only. In the current configuration, this includes random brightness, contrast, and gamma perturbations, Gaussian blur, and additive image noise.

supervision applied to the standard Dreamer feature

f

IV-E
Training Protocol
All agents are trained exclusively on source-town experience. In the current multi-town training setup, source data are collected from Town05 and Town06, and the held-out towns are excluded from gradient updates throughout training. For methods that use town supervision, the source-domain labels are defined by the source-town identity, i.e., Town05 and Town06 correspond to the two supervised town classes used during training.
All compared methods share the same base DreamerV3-style optimization pipeline, including the same world-model backbone, replay mechanism, imagined-rollout actor–critic updates, camera preprocessing, discrete action interface, and episode termination rules. The methods differ only in the auxiliary components enabled
for training: RGB augmentation, semantic projection and context branch, semantic rollout supervision, town-adversarial supervision, and context-side town supervision. This controlled protocol ensures that performance differences are attributable to the proposed regularization mechanisms rather than to unrelated changes in optimizer or simulator configuration.
Each method is trained from scratch under three random seeds. In the current training script, the seeds are

, and each seed corresponds to a separate run with its own log directory and checkpoint history. For methods using semantic rollout supervision, replayed transitions additionally carry frozen OpenCLIP image embeddings. For methods using town supervision, replayed transitions carry the corresponding source-town label and domain mask. Methods that do not use a given auxiliary signal are trained with the corresponding inputs and losses disabled.

IV-F
Evaluation Protocol and Metrics
Evaluation is performed in closed loop on held-out target-town routes without any target-domain adaptation, fine-tuning, or test-time update. Each trained model is evaluated for 64 closed-loop episodes per held-out town under the same simulator and control settings used during training. For each episode, one route is sampled uniformly from the corresponding 8-route held-out evaluation pool. When multiple held-out towns are considered, results are reported separately for each target town.
An evaluation episode terminates when the agent reaches the goal, collides, remains persistently off-road, remains persistently in the wrong lane, becomes stuck, passes beyond the goal, or reaches the timeout horizon. In the current
environment, success is defined as reaching the goal within a
2
​
m
2\,\mathrm{m}
radius, and the timeout horizon is
2000
2000
control steps.
We report two groups of metrics. The first group captures task completion and safety:
•

[km over 64 eval episodes]: total distance traveled by the ego vehicle accumulated over all 64 evaluation episodes of a seed; the paper reports mean ± std across seeds;
•

•
Collisions/km
: total number of collision events divided by the total traveled distance in kilometers;
•
Lane invasions/km
: total number of lane-invasion events divided by the total traveled distance in kilometers.
The second group captures lane-keeping and speed behavior:
•
Lane invasions/100 m
: total number of lane-invasion events normalized by traveled distance in units of

•
Off-centre error
[m]: mean lateral deviation from the reference lane/path, averaged over time within each episode and then over episodes;
•
Heading error
[rad]: mean heading deviation from the reference direction, averaged over time within each episode and then over episodes;
•
Mean speed
[km/h]: episode-averaged vehicle speed, averaged over evaluation episodes;
•
Max speed
[km/h]: episode-wise maximum speed, averaged over evaluation episodes.
All reported values are first computed for each independently trained seed over the 64 sampled evaluation episodes of each held-out town, and the paper reports the mean and standard deviation across training seeds. For distance-normalized event rates, event counts and traveled distance are accumulated over all evaluation episodes of a seed before normalization. This avoids distortions caused by averaging per-episode ratios with widely different path lengths.
In the reported experiments, each seed is evaluated for 64 closed-loop episodes per held-out town. These episodes are generated by uniformly sampling from the 8 fixed held-out routes of that town.

V-D
Discussion
Across both held-out towns, the most defensible conclusion from the current study is that combining semantic rollout supervision with domain-adversarial latent regularization improves zero-shot held-out-town route-following performance more reliably than standard Dreamer training, image
augmentation alone, or domain-adversarial training alone. The main result is higher mean held-out-town success within the evaluated protocol, not uniform superiority on every safety or lane-keeping metric. This advantage is expressed most clearly in successful closed-loop route completion on unseen town layouts, while the lane-keeping statistics further suggest improved route adherence on the harder held-out town. At the same time, the proposed method does not dominate every secondary
driving metric on every town, so the evidence supports a measured improvement in transfer performance rather than uniform superiority across all operating points.
The scope of this conclusion should remain explicit. The present experiments isolate fixed-weather town shift in a controlled CARLA route-following setting and do not test broader sources of distribution shift such as dynamic traffic,
pedestrians, or weather variation. Accordingly, the results should be interpreted as evidence for improved held-out-town generalization within this benchmark, not as a general claim of robust autonomous driving under all out-of-distribution
conditions.
V-E
Limitations and Threats to Validity
This study has several important limitations. First, the evaluation isolates structural cross-town variation under fixed ClearNoon weather and without traffic or pedestrians, so the results do not establish robustness to dynamic agents or weather shift. Second, the task is route-unconditioned: the policy receives no navigation command, route polyline, goal pose, or map input, and the reference route is used only by the environment for reward and termination. Third, all methods observe collision and lane-invasion event bits as part of the common observation interface. Finally, the study uses only two source towns and two held-out towns. The findings should therefore be interpreted as evidence for improved transfer within this controlled CARLA benchmark rather than as a general autonomous-driving claim.
A further limitation is that the study does not include broader cross-family baselines such as command-conditioned imitation-learning systems or planner-based CARLA pipelines. This omission is intentional and reflects the question being asked: the present paper tests whether semantic rollout supervision and town-adversarial regularization improve held-out-town transfer within a fixed Dreamer-style route-unconditioned agent, not whether that agent family dominates other driving paradigms that use different conditioning signals, supervision, or task definitions. A broader cross-family study would be valuable, but making it fair would require either harmonizing those additional inputs and supervision across methods or accepting a less controlled comparison with multiple simultaneous confounds.
VI
Conclusion
This paper studied zero-shot held-out-town transfer for route-unconditioned fixed-route closed-loop driving in CARLA under a deliberately controlled protocol. Training was performed only on Town05 and Town06, while evaluation was carried out zero-shot on held-out Town03 and Town04 under fixed weather and without traffic or pedestrians. Within a DreamerV3-style world-model agent, we introduced two training-time auxiliary losses: multi-horizon prediction of frozen OpenCLIP image embeddings along imagined rollouts, and town-adversarial supervision on a semantic projection of the deterministic RSSM state. A history-conditioned context feature was used to condition the semantic rollout predictor, while the actor and critic retained the standard Dreamer feature so that the semantic/context branch acted as an auxiliary regularizer rather than replacing the control input.
Across both held-out towns, the combined Sem+Adv-Aux model achieved the highest mean route success among the compared Dreamer-family methods. Taken together, the results indicate that combining semantic rollout supervision with town-adversarial regularization can improve mean held-out-town transfer in this controlled CARLA setting. At the same time, the evidence is deliberately bounded: the gains are not uniform across all secondary safety and lane-keeping metrics, and the proposed model should not be interpreted as dominating every aspect of closed-loop driving performance.
The present study should therefore be read as a focused simulation-based analysis rather than as evidence of broad autonomous-driving robustness or as a cross-paradigm CARLA ranking. The experiments do not establish robustness to weather variation, background traffic, pedestrians, sensor corruption, or real-world deployment, and they do not address richer command-conditioned or map-conditioned driving settings. Future work should test whether the same auxiliary design remains useful under broader sources of distribution shift, larger sets of held-out towns and routes, and stronger cross-family baselines evaluated under a carefully harmonized protocol.

received the B.E. degree in computer systems engineering from Mehran University of Engineering and Technology, Pakistan, and the M.S. degree in computer and information engineering. She is currently a graduate student research assistant and Ph.D. candidate at the University of Michigan–Dearborn. Her research interests include robotics, autonomous vehicles, deep learning for perception and control, probabilistic modeling, reinforcement learning, and robust decision-making in complex environments. She previously held research positions at Freie Universität Berlin and the University of Bath, working on machine learning, computer vision, and intelligent systems. Prior to her academic research roles, she was a software programmer with Fateh Motors Ltd., where she contributed to software development and system integration.
Jaerock Kwon
(Senior Member, IEEE) received the B.S. and M.S. degrees in Electronic Communication Engineering from Hanyang University, Seoul, South Korea, in 1992 and 1994, respectively, and the Ph.D. degree in Computer Engineering from Texas A&M University, College Station, USA, in 2009. From 1994 to 2004, he worked at LG Electronics, SK Teletech, and Qualcomm Internet Services. From 2009 to 2010, he was a Professor at the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Kettering University, Flint, MI, USA. preview_text:
Learned driving agents often degrade when deployed in unseen environments. This paper studies a deliberately bounded instance of that problem in the CARLA simulator: zero-shot transfer of a closed-loop fixed-route driving agent from Town05 and Town06 to unseen Town03 and Town04. The study isolates structural town shift by keeping weather fixed to ClearNoon and removing traffic and pedestrians. We build on a Dreamer-style latent world-model agent and add two training-only auxiliary losses: multi-horizon prediction of future visual-semantic embeddings along imagined rollouts and town-adversarial supervision on a semantic projection of the recurrent latent state. A causal context feature conditions the semantic rollout predictor, while the actor and critic retain the standard control feature. The policy receives no navigation command, route polyline, goal pose, or map input; the reference route is used only by the environment for reward, progress, success, and termination. Across the evaluated held-out towns, the proposed model achieves the highest mean success rate among the included Dreamer-family methods. Secondary safety and lane-keeping metrics are mixed across towns. These results support a bounded conclusion: in this controlled fixed-weather CARLA setting, semantic rollout supervision combined with town-adversarial regularization improves mean held-out-town route completion.

Dreaming Across Towns: Semantic Rollout and Town-Adversarial Regularization for Zero-Shot Held-Out-Town Fixed-Route Driving in CARLA
